coredata.dc:title: One-step hydrothermal synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon-quantum-dots for detection and efficient removal of high-concentrations nicotine from tobacco wastewater under visible light

coredata.dc:description: Monitoring and implementing nicotine wastewater treatment with a rapid and feasible method to benefit the clean water environment. We successfully synthesized nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots photocatalysts (N-CQDs) via an efficient hydrothermal synthesis strategy for fluorescence detection and photocatalytic degradation of nicotine wastewater. Transmission electron microscope confirmed nearly spherical in shape of N-CQDs. X-ray diffraction study endorsed the formation of N-CQDs. The as-fabricated N-CQDs showed high sensitivity and good linear range (1–50 mM) for the detection of nicotine wastewater. The LOD and LOQ values were calculated as 0.89 mM and 2.71 mM. The catalyst photodecomposed 99.69 % of nicotine wastewater at 90 min. Under solar light irradiation, the degradation rate of nicotine was improved with the increasing in concentration, ·O2 –, h+, 1O2 and ·OH were the main active species. Furthermore, the photocatalytic degradation pathway and the detection mechanism of nicotine by N-CQDs were explored.

head.ce:title: One-step hydrothermal synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon-quantum-dots for detection and efficient removal of high-concentrations nicotine from tobacco wastewater under visible light

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Monitoring and implementing nicotine wastewater treatment with a rapid and feasible method to benefit the clean water environment. We successfully synthesized nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots photocatalysts (N-CQDs) via an efficient hydrothermal synthesis strategy for fluorescence detection and photocatalytic degradation of nicotine wastewater. Transmission electron microscope confirmed nearly spherical in shape of N-CQDs. X-ray diffraction study endorsed the formation of N-CQDs. The as-fabricated N-CQDs showed high sensitivity and good linear range (1–50 mM) for the detection of nicotine wastewater. The LOD and LOQ values were calculated as 0.89 mM and 2.71 mM. The catalyst photodecomposed 99.69 % of nicotine wastewater at 90 min. Under solar light irradiation, the degradation rate of nicotine was improved with the increasing in concentration, ·O2 –, h+, 1O2 and ·OH were the main active species. Furthermore, the photocatalytic degradation pathway and the detection mechanism of nicotine by N-CQDs were explored.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Nicotine is a toxic alkaloid found in the tobacco plant (at a lethal dose of 60 mg in adults and is the most important ingredient in tobacco products) [1–3]. Mass consumption of tobacco products leads to widespread nicotine contamination in surface water, groundwater and especially in wastewater [3,4]. Sewage from urban wastewater treatment plants is one of main point sources. Nicotine has been reported to be presented at concentrations in the range of nanograms per liter to micrograms per liter [3]. Moreover, nicotine and its metabolites are used as chemical markers for domestic wastewater contamination and for population size assessment [1,3]. Therefore, the detection and removal of nicotine in water environment is particularly important.

At present, the commonly-used nicotine detection methods mainly include electrochemical [5], liquid/gas chromatography [6], capillary electrophoresis [7], colorimetric assay [8] and fluorescence sensor [9]. Among them, liquid/gas chromatography and capillary electrophoresis require sophisticated and expensive instruments, cannot achieve real-time detection, and require extensive purification stages as well as highly trained operators to operate them. Due to low cost and rapid response sensitivity, electrochemical and fluorescent sensors have received much attention. Existing nicotine wastewater treatment methods include physical methods [10], biological methods [11] and chemical methods [12]. Physical method is mainly distillation, which is difficult to deal with, high energy consumption and operation complexity. Biological methods include activated sludge and biofilm, always require a complex biological environment, such as certain nutrients, pH, temperature and other conditions. Chemical methods are mainly based on the redox system [13], which includes Fenton oxidation [14], ozone catalytic oxidation [15], electrochemical oxidation [16] and photocatalytic oxidation [17–19]. Among them, the reaction conditions of Fenton system are difficult to control, the output of iron mud is large, easy to recolor; the running cost of ozone oxidation is high, easy to produce harmful by-products, and low utilization rate; electrochemical oxidation consumes more electricity and electrode metals with high cost; photocatalytic oxidation has been widely concerned by the people due to taking light as energy, green environmental protection, energy saving and high efficiency, no secondary pollution.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as a new star of luminous materials, has been widely applied in the field of photocatalytic [20–22] and optical sensors due to their unique optical and electronic characteristics, such as excellent sunlight harvesting capability, tunable photoluminescence (PL), up-conversion photoluminescence (UCPL), and efficient photoexcited electron transfer. The UCPL of CQDs can take advantage of the full spectrum of solar light possible to enhance light absorption and thus photogenerates electrons and holes [23–26] which help produce active species to efficiently degrade pollutants. Besides, CQDs has the unique advantages of helical binding photoluminescence (PL), large stokes shift, good photochemical stability, adjustable emission wavelength and excellent biological compatibility, which can be used in fluorescence detection technology [27,28]. Hence, we can design and construct a stable and efficient CQDs photoactive semiconductor materials to detect and degrade nicotine. However, due to the poor activity under visible light and the recombination of photoelectron and hole pairs, CQDs is currently rarely used in the photocatalysis field, and the degradation mechanism is still ambiguous. Moreover, CQDs usually has few studies on pollutant detection, and how to use CQD for pollutant detection becomes extremely challenging.

Herein, we construct N-CQDs via a facile hydrothermal treatment of folic acid (FA) and o-phenylenediamine (oPD) with 95 % ethanol as assistant agents. N-CQDs was characterized by analytical techniques including TEM, XRD, FTIR, DRS, and XPS. The exciton dissociation and carrier migration behavior of N-CQDs were investigated using fluorescence techniques and photochemical measurements. We studied the fluorescence absorption, fluorescence emission and transient fluorescence spectra and elucidated the detection mechanism. The limit of detection and sensitivity of detecting nicotine were explored using fluorescence spectroscopy. The photoactivity of the catalyst against nicotine degradation was investigated. EPR tests and free radical trapping experiments were performed to elucidate the degradation mechanism. The degradation process of the catalyst was examined using LC-MS to identify intermediates and degradation pathways. This study investigated the effects of different concentrations of nicotine and catalysts, different pH and different contaminants on nicotine degradation and the effects of different anions and different contaminants on nicotine detection. The objective of the study is to provide a new strategy for the detection and degradation of nicotine pollution in the water environment.

The N-CQDs was prepared via a hydrothermal method. First, 0.5 g FA and 0.5 g oPD were dispersed in 10 mL 95 % ethanol solution. Then the dispersed solution was treated at 180 °C for 1 h in a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. Finally, the products were collected by centrifugation, washed alternately 5 times using 95 % ethanol and water, and then dried by freeze dryer. The preparation process of N-CQDs was depicted in Fig. 1
(a).

The morphology and microstructure images were received by transmission electron microscope (TEM) and high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) graph (JEOL, JEM-2010). UV–visible diffuse reflection spectra (UV–vis DRS) was tested from 200 to 800 nm using a Hitachi UH4150 spectrometer. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was obtained by Thermo Nicolet 5700 analyzer. The chemical environment of the photocatalyst was collected by x-ray photoelectron spectrum (XPS) (Thermo Fisher Scientific) with a monochromatic Al-Kα source at 1486.6 eV. Powder x-ray diffraction (XRD) was utilized to explore the crystal structure of the photocatalyst in the range of 2θ from 5° to 75° though a Bruker AXS D8-discover diffractometer operating with Cu-Kα radiationin. Fluorescence spectra (FL) was gained in the range of 200 to 900 nm using the F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer of Hitachi, Japan. The FL lifetimes were measured using an Edinburg FLS-980 time-resolved fluorescence spectrometer. The thermal stability energy of the sample was analyzed by TGA (METTLER TOLEDO, TGA1) under N2 atmosphere. An electrochemical workstation CHI660E (Chenhua Instrument) was used to determine the photoelectric properties with Pt wire as the counter electrode, an Ag/AgCl wire (saturated KCl solution) as reference electrode, the samples on carbon paper as the working electrode using 0.1 M Na2SO4 as an electrolyte solution in three-electrode cell system. The possible active species under solar light irradiation were determined using a electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) technology (Bruker A-300). The content of organic elements was obtained by using an organic element analyzer (Elementar UNICUBE).

An Xe-1 test chamber from Q-Sun (USA) was used to evaluate photocatalytic performance of samples by using nicotine as the target pollutant. To the glass beaker, 20 mL of nicotine aqueous solution (2 × 10−5 mol/L) containing 2 mg photocatalyst (0.1 g/L) was transferred. In the dark, the uniform suspension was obtained by ultrasonication for 10 min before solar light irradiation. At a certain time interval, 1.0 mL solution after the reaction was extracted and photocatalyst particle was removed through a 0.22 μm Teflon filter membrane. A PDA detector was used to monitore the nicotine concentration by Ultra-performance liquid chromatography (UPLC, Waters).

For sensing of nicotine, to the glass beaker, 100 mL of Millipore water aqueous solution containing 10 mg N-CQDs (0.1 g/L) was transferred. The uniform suspension was obtained by ultrasonication for 10 min. Then the nicotine stock solution (4–208 μL) was added to the sonicated suspension to make a nicotine solution (1–50 μM) containing N-CQDs. The FL spectra were recorded at 400 nm for N-CQD. The absorption spectra were also recorded in the range of 360 to 900 nm.

UPLC/HDMS (Waters, Synapt G2-S) with an electrospray ionization (ESI) source was applied to detect the degradation intermediates and final products of nicotine over N-CQDs. Relevant components were analyzed at a flow rate of 0.3 mL/min by HSS T3 column (1.8 μm, 2.1 × 100 mm). In positive mode, the mobile phase consisted of aqueous ammonia solution with volume fraction of 0.1 % (solvent A) and acetonitrile (solvent B). During the separation, the intermediates of nicotine degradation was explored by an elution isocratic schedule of VA:VB=30:70 and the injection volume was 5 μL.

The morphology structure and the size distribution of photocatalyst N-CQDs were investigated by TEM. N-CQDs exhibits a diminutive size, predominantly spherical morphology, and monodispersity as depicted in Fig. 1 (b). The size distribution of N-CQDs is presented in the accompanying histogram, revealing an average size of 3.41±0.79 nm for the synthesized N-CQDs. Furthermore, defects of N-CQDs may be formed due to the interaction of FA and oPD in a high-temperature closed reactor [28]. The high-resolution TEM images of N-CQDs are shown in Fig. 1 (c), it can be observed that the interlayer spacing of the N-CQDs is about 0.24 nm, agreeing well with the (002) spacing of graphitic carbon [29]. The crystallization property of the N-CQDs is studied by electron diffraction. The lattice spacing of the N-CQDs is about 2.375 Å. The result agrees with the carbon's (002) diffraction plane (JCPDS 37–1464).

The phases and structures of samples were identified by XRD. According to Fig. 2
(a), XRD patterns of N-CQDs are consistent with the literatures [31,32]. Diffraction peaks at 21.8° [33] and 26.7° [34] are correspond to the (220) and (002) planes of CQDs (JCPDS 87–1526), respectively. It is attributed to the graphitic-like layered stacking of CN-based materials. The absence of these two distinct diffraction peaks in FA, oPD and oPD+FA suggest that they could not form the N-CQD structure. On the other hand, the peaks assign to (220) and (002) of N-CQDs gradually take shape as the increase of reaction temperature, especially the temperature exceeds 160℃. It should be noticed that once the reaction temperature is below 160℃, the synthesized samples display numerous erratic peaks, which is undesirable for the N-CQDs. Additionally, reaction times of samples were also explored. Fig. 2 (b) shows that the (220) and (002) crystal planes of N-CQDs can be observed in all samples. This indicates that the CQDs may grow very quickly when the reaction temperature is high enough, making the effect of the reaction time become insignificant. Based on these results, the reaction temperature was the critical factor, which influenced the structure formation of N-CQDs.

Thermogravimetric stability was examined by TGA (Fig. 2 (c)). In Stage 1, the thermal decomposition temperature of N-CQDs is approximately 50℃. The thermal decomposition rate of N-CQDs reaches its maximum at about 65℃ with the rise of temperature. The water of N-CQDs is basically evaporated completely when the temperature hit 85℃. At this stage, the evaporation rate of water is 0.05 %/℃, and the total mass loss is 2.37 %. In Stage 2, the thermal decomposition temperature of N-CQDs is approximately 260℃. The thermal decomposition rate of N-CQDs reaches its maximum at about 600℃ with the rise of temperature. The N-CQDs has essentially become carbonized when the temperature hit 750℃, the sample mass loss has gradually stabilized and no considerable change any longer. At this stage, the carbonization rate of the N-CQDs is 0.068 %/℃, and the total mass loss is 48.07 %. The result of TGA demonstrated that N-CQDs had excellent thermal stability.

The visible light absorption properties of the samples were investigated using DRS spectroscopy. In Fig. 2 (d), the edges of the light absorption peaks for oPD and FA appear at 340 nm and 550 nm, respectively, and the edge of pure N-CQDs appears at approximately 500 nm. Comparing with oPD, N-CQDs shows redshift to longer wavelength range and stronger visible light absorption capacity, indicating that the introduction of FA broadens the visible light absorption range of N-CQDs and improves the visible light utilization of N-CQDs, which is more favorable to the photocatalytic reactions.

The chemical structures of prepared samples were illustrated by FT-IR (Fig. 2 (e)). In the FT-IR spectrum of N-CQDs, the intensive band in the region of 3300–3470 cm−1 is indicated of the absorption by stretching vibration of non-condensed terminal NH2 or NH groups and O-H [35]. Specifically, O-H is derived from FA and N-CQDs, and N-H is related to oPD, FA and N-CQDs. The absorption peak at 1625 cm−1 may be ascribed to the stretching vibration of C=O [36], which is came from FA and the N-CQDs. The characteristic peak at 1498 cm−1 is caused by the bond stretching modes of the sp2 hybrid's C=C [37]. The characteristic peak at 1224 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of the C-O [38], which is obtained from the FA and N-CQDs. The N-CQDs is shown to be cofunctionalized by weak electron-withdrawing carboxyl groups produced by FA and strong electron-donating groups (such as amino groups and OH) produced by oPD and ethanol [39], respectively.

The chemical state and composition of the as-prepared catalyst were explored by XPS analysis. The N-CQDs is primarily made up of C, N and O elements (Fig. 2 (f)). Three characteristic peaks are visible in the entire spectra of the N-CQD samples, with energies of 285.11 eV, 399.23 eV and 531.34 eV for C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively. Furthermore, the mass percentages of N, C, H, and O in the sample are 23.79 %, 52.08 %, 4.81 %, and 21.62 %, respectively, according to the analysis results of organic elements content ( Table S1 ). The C1s spectra of pure N-CQDs can be divided into four species at 284.80, 286.08, 288.56 and 291.03 eV, which are ascribed to C═C/C─C, C─C/C─O, carboxyl O─C═O and N─C═N [38], respectively (Fig. 2 (g)). The full N1s band displays two peaks at 399.04 eV and 400.27 eV that are derived from N─H and C─N/C═N [40], respectively(Fig. 2 (h)). For the O1s spectra, two peaks of 531.04 and 532.92 eV, ascribing to C═O and the surface hydroxyl group (C─O─H) group of C atomic bonds [39], respectively (Fig. 2 (i)).

Photocatalytic activity of the prepared catalysts was evaluated by the nicotine degradation efficiency under solar light irradiation (Fig. 3 ). Firstly, an appropriate ratio between oPD and FA is beneficial to improve the photocatalytic activity. The degradation efficiencies of nicotine at 150 min are 55.4 %, 75.3 %, 85.5 %, and 77.2 % when oPD and FA ratios are 0:1, 0.5:1, 1:1 and 2:1. It can be seen the oPD and FA ratio 1:1 is chosen for further study (Fig. 3 (a)). Supplementarily, pure oPD got no product after hydrothermal. In addition, according to the previous characterization, it is known that the reaction temperature affects the structure of N-CQDs, degradation activities of synthesized catalysts at different temperatures are tested. The degradation efficiency of nicotine at 150 min are 50.4 %, 83.1 %, 92.2 % and 86.5 % when reaction temperatures are 140℃, 160℃, 180℃ and 200℃ (Fig. 3 (b)). FA and oPD can not synthesize N-CQDs when the reaction temperature is 140℃ (Fig. 2 (a)), so their photocatalytic removal of nicotine is very low. However, N-CQDs synthesized at 160℃, 180℃ and 200℃ significantly improves the photocatalytic degradation of nicotine, indicating that the N-CQDs structure responds well to light and improves the separation efficiency of electron hole pairs. At 160℃, the N-CQDs structure is just formed, with fewer defects and poor photocatalytic effect; at 180℃, abundant N-CQDs structure is formed and abundant defect sites are generated, and the photocatalytic effect is good. At 200℃, the N-CQDs structure is partially carbonized, while the defect site is reduced and the photocatalytic effect is poor. Among them, the number of defect sites can affect the separation efficiency of electron-hole pairs, thus affecting the photocatalytic degradation effect of N-CQDs for nicotine. It can be seen 180℃ is chosen for further study. Noting that although the reaction time has no effect on the structure of the N-CQDs, the activity experiment suggests the degradation efficiency of nicotine at 150 min are 72.9 %, 90.2 %, 90.7 %, 92.1 %, 90.2 % and 91.2 %, when reaction times are 0.5 h, 1 h, 2 h, 4 h, 8 h and 12 h (Fig. 3 (c)). It can be seen that the degradation efficiency of 1–12 h are similar. For the sake of energy conservation, 1 h is selected for further study. In Fig. 3 (d), the degradation rate of nicotine is almost constant in the absence of a photocatalyst, indicating that the nicotine can not be degraded under solar light. Besides, N-CQDs has no catalytic activity without a light source. In the presence of a light source and a catalyst, the degradation efficiency of nicotine reachs 83 %. This reveals that catalytic oxidation reaction must be driven by a light source. Under solar light, N-CQDs possesses a higher photocatalytic oxidation capacity and can efficiently degrade the nicotine pollutants in wastewater.

Fig. 3 (e) depicts the result of a study on the photocatalytic activity of N-CQD catalysts at four distinct nicotine concentrations: 0.06 mM, 0.12 mM, 0.24 mM, and 0.48 mM. It is evident that when the concentration of the nicotine solution increases, so does the N-CQDs catalyst photocatalytic activity. This proves that the N-CQDs is an effective photocatalyst for the removal of high concentrations nicotine. To determine the catalyst stability before evaluating its potential in real-world applications is important. Six cycle studies with N-CQDs are conducted under uniform conditions. The N-CQDs is no discernible inactivation after recovering from 6 consecutive cycles of deterioration under sunlight (150 min per run), which demonstrates the chemical stability and dependability of N-CQDs (Fig. 3 (f)).

To gauge the improvements provided by N-CQDs for nicotine degradation, we compared the efficiency of nicotine degradation by different methods and by different materials. Fig. S1 (a) shows the efficiency of different methods for treating nicotine wastewater. It can be seen that the photocatalytic oxidation method has the highest efficiency, the shortes time and the best effect, followed by biological method, then physical method. Among them, the efficiency of the biological method and physical method is referenced in the previous literature [10,11]. In nicotine wastewater treatment, g-C3N4 and its composites show excellent property [41,42]. Compared with other materials, g-C3N4 exhibits enhanced photoabsorption capacity and higher photocatalytic activity based on its unique optical and chemical properties [43,44]. Here, the photocatalytic degradation performance of nicotine between g-C3N4 and N-CQD are compared, and the results are shown in Fig. S1 (b). The degradation efficiency of nicotine by N-CQDs is far higher than the g-C3N4, and the performance improvement is about six times higher, which reveals the superior of N-CQDs for nicotine degradation.

To confirm the practicability of N-CQDs for the nicotine degradation, the analysis was performed in real samples, and the results are shown in Table S3 . 25−100 mM nicotine was spiked in the real (river and pond water) samples with good relative standard deviation (lower than 2.21 %) were obtained. Therefore, N-CQDs have potential applicability for nicotine degradation in real samples.

To deep explore the photocatalytic selectivity of N-CQDs for nicotine degradation, carbamazepine (CBZ), sulfachlorpyridazine (SCP) and Sulfadiazine (SD) were used as interfering pollutants to further investigate the photocatalytic activity of N-CQDs for nicotine degradation under visible light(Fig. S1 (c)). It can be seen that the nicotine degradation efficiency of N-CQDs is largely unchanged after the addition of 25 µM CBZ, 12.5 µM CBZ+10 µM SCP, and 10 µM CBZ+of 12.5 µM SCP+10 µM SD. The result shows that N-CQDs is highly selective for nicotine degradation in the presence of typical interfering pollutants, providing selective nicotine degradation. The photocatalytic degradation efficiency of nicotine in the N-CQDs system is probed at different pH values since the reaction kinetics of catalytic oxidation are often affected by pH. The nicotine removal rates are still relatively high in a wide pH range. In contrast, N-CQDs exhibits a better photocatalytic degradation activity against nicotine in alkaline and neutral conditions than in acidic condition (Fig. S1 (d)).

To understand the mechanism of nicotine degradation, EPR spin-trap technology test was used to monitore the possible reactive radicals produced by N-CQDs in the light reaction. DMPO as a spin capture reagent is used to capture ·OH and ·O2 – [45,46]. As shown in Fig. 4
(a), ·OH signal is observed after irradiating the water-dispersion liquid of N-CQDs for 2 min. Hence, we consider that ·OH is a specie in the N-CQDs photocatalytic reaction system. In Fig. 4 (b), the involvement of 1O2 is also affirmed under solar light irradiation by 2,2,6,6-Tetramethylpiperidin-1-oxyl (TEMP) as the spin-trapping reagent with an intensity ratio of 1:1:1 [47]. In Fig. 4 (c), Four characteristic peaks of DMPO-·O2 – are detected in the N-CQDs system under solar light irradiation. ·O2 – is produced by a single-electron reduction process. The EPR studies revealed that 1O2 and ·O2 – werethe dominant active species generated in this catalytic system. The EPR of N-CQDs solution with varied nicotine concentrations is investigated for ·O2 – and 1O2 in Fig. 4 (d)(e). The ·O2 – and 1O2 signals rapidly enhances with nicotine concentration increases, futher indicating that ·O2 – and 1O2 indeed are the dominant active species in photocatalysis.

h+ is generally considered as the other dominant active species of N-CQDs. Thereby, radicals capture experiments were performed using KI, p-benzoquinone (BQ), L-Histidine and tert.-butyl alcohol (TBA) as h+, ·O2 – and 1O2, 1O2 and ·OH removers, respectively (Fig. 4 (f)). In the presence of KI, the photocatalytic degradation of nicotine is significantly inhibited, indicating that h+ is another main active specie during photocatalytic process. Similarly, the degradation efficiency of nicotine decreases after the addition of 1 mM BQ, indicating that ·O2 – and 1O2 are active species in photocatalysis. The degradation efficiency of nicotine decreases after the addition of 1 mM L-Histidine, again indicating that 1O2 is indeed active species in photocatalysis. After the addition of 10 mM TBA, the photocatalytic degradation activity of nicotine first increases and then remains consistent with the control group. This indicates that ·OH is produced in the second half of the photocatalytic degradation of N-CQDs, and their effect is weak. In addition, the kinetic study on the capture experiment was performed ( Fig. S2 ). The results show that ln(C/C0) and reaction time (t) follow the first-order kinetic pattern ln(C/C0) = -kt. It is well known that higher k value indicate faster response. As can be seen that k(BQ) < k(KI) < k(L-Histidine) < k(TBA) < k(No addition), indicating that the contribution degree of free radicals is·O2 –>h+>1O2>·OH. Therefore, in the photocatalytic system of N-CQDs, ∙O2 – is the main active species, followed by h+, 1O2 and·OH. Consequently, ·O2 –, h+, 1O2 and·OH work together in the reaction system to promote the photocatalytic degradation of nicotine, which is a good agreement with the above EPR results.

To exclude other mechanisms that might lead to degradation, the characterization analysis of the N-CQDs after adsorption, after degradation, and after washing were performed. Fig. S3 (a) shows the nicotine content of the N-CQDs system after adsorption, after degradation and after washing. It can be seen that the nicotine content is 100 % after dsorption, 16.79 % after degradation, and 0.87 % after washing. This indicates that the removal of nicotine by N-CQDs is mainly through degradation, with a very weak adsorption effect of only 0.87 %. Fig. S3 (b) shows the FT-IR spectra of N-CQDs and N-CQDs after washing. The characteristic absorption peak of C=O of N-CQDs after washing disappeared and the absorption peak intensity of N-H/O-H and C=C decreased. This suggests that oxygen-containing functional groups such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface of N-CQDs may be consumed by electron transfer or free radical reactions during photocatalysis. Furthermore, the sp2 hybrid carbon structure in N-CQDs may bind with nicotine through π-π interaction. During photocatalysis, this interaction may be disrupted, leading to changes in the corresponding absorption peaks in the FT-IR spectra. The mineralization capacity of N-CQDs is listed in Table S2 . It can be seen that the total organic carbon content of N-CQDs is significantly reduced, which indicates that nicotine is not only adsorbed but also mineralized into inorganic substances.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FRET-based fluorescence quenching of N-CQDs by nicotine
ce:section.ce:para:
In Fig. 5
(a), the emission spectra of N-CQDs peaks at around 806 nm and the absorption spectrum of nicotine is between 790 and 820 nm. The fluorescence emission spectrum of N-CQDs (donor) overlaps with the absorption spectrum of nicotine (acceptor), which meets the condition of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [48,49]. If the fluorescence emission spectrum of one fluorescent group (donor) overlaps with the absorption spectrum of the other group (receptor) and the distance between the two groups is appropriate (typically less than 50 nm), the transfer of fluorescence energy from donor to acceptor, namely donor fluorescence quenching and receptor fluorescence enhancement, is referred to as fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). Under weak acids, N-CQDs (energy donor) and nicotine (energy receptor) built a new type of energy transfer system, fluorescence energy can be transferred from the N-CQDs to the nicotine. Therefore, we can apply the FRET process of N-CQDs to the fluorescence detection of nicotine. As shown in Fig. 6
(b), the fluorescence intensity of the system is enhancing with the increase of nicotine concentration when N-CQDs concentration is fixed. Fig. 6 (c) shows a good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of the system and nicotine concentration, which is due to more energy is transferred to the acceptor, confirming the establishment of the fluorescence resonance energy system, providing the conditions for nicotine detection. In Fig. 6 (d), there is an increase of nicotine fluorescence intensity and the occurrence of corresponding N-CQDs fluorescence quenching phenomenon when the concentration of nicotine is fixed and the concentration of N-CQDs is changed. Fig. 6 (e) shows a good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of the system and catalyst content. This is because more donors transfered the energy to the acceptor, again confirming the existence of a fluorescence resonance system, further providing support for nicotine analysis. The time-resolved fluorescence decay of N-CQDs reveals that the radiative recombination of the excitons gives rise to fluorescence (Fig. 5 (f)). The average lifetime of N-CQDs is found to be 2.45 ns, which decreases to 1.82 ns in the presence of nicotine. The energy transfer efficiency (E) value is calculated to be E=0.2571 from eq 1 using lifetime values of donor (τD) and donor−acceptor (τD–A) species [48]. Thus, the FRET efficiency is obtained as 25.71 %.

Effective fluorescent quenching of N-CQDs was observed by the interaction between N-CQDs and nicotine. In Fig. 6 (a), the fluorescence intensity of the system increases significantly as nicotine is added to the system. Fig. 6 (b) shows that the fluorescence intensity of the system is well linear with the nicotine concentration, with a linear regression range of 1–50 mM, a LOD of 0.89 mM and a LOQ of 2.71 mM. Meanwhile, the fluorescence resonance efficiency shows a good linear relationship with the nicotine concentration and a maximum fluorescence resonance efficiency of 93.3 % is obtained (Fig. 6 (c)).

Selectivity is one of the most important analytical factors in practical applications of nicotine determination. Fig. S4 (a) shows the fluorescence response of N-CQDs to the addition of 0.06 mM nicotine, 25 µM CBZ, 12.5 µM CBZ+12.5 µM SCP, 10 µM CBZ+10 µM SCP+10 µM SD, and a second addition of 0.06 mM nicotine. It can be seen that the change of the fluorescence response is little after adding the interfering pollutants, indicating that the prepared N-CQDs has good selectivity for nicotine. Fig. S4 (b) shows the fluorescence response of N-CQDs to the addition of 0.06 mM nicotine, 0.05 mM NaCl, 0.05 mM Na2SO4, 0.05 mM Na2CO3, 0.05 mM NaNO3, and a second addition of 0.06 mM nicotine. The change of the fluorescence response is little after adding the interfering anions, again indicating that the prepared N-CQDs has good selectivity for nicotine. The result showed that N-CQDs exhibited high selectivity for nicotine in the presence of high concentrations of typical interfering anions and interfering pollutants, which could provide selective nicotine detection.

The photocurrent-time measurement was considered as an efficient method to verify the separation efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. As shown in Fig. 7
a), oPD exhibits the best photocurrent density response under light irradiation. This further demonstrates that the introduction of oPD can efficiently separate the photo-generated electron-hole pairs of N-CQDs compared to FA. Furthermore, the inclusion of oPD structure can greatly lower the resistance to electron transport, according to the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurement (Fig. 7 (b)). This significantly aids N-CQD electron transport during photocatalysis.

Valence band (VB) and conduction band (CB) of the N-CQDs were investigate by XPS (Fig. 7 (c)). It is discovered that the VB value of N-CQDs is 1.68 eV. DRS was used to determine band gap of N-CQDs (Fig. 7 (d)). Band gap width of N-CQDs is 2.48 eV after calculation. Band gap energy of N-CQDs can also be calculated using eq 2 [39], where λedge is the wavelength of the maximum absorption edge.
Eg = 1240/λedge (2)

To better probe the relationship between fluorescence quenching and photocatalytic degradation, electron transport and radical reactions after washing N-CQDs and N-CQDs, and trapping agent experiments after washing N-CQDs and their kinetic parameters were studied. It can be seen that the washed N-CQDs shows faster electron transport and more radical generation, which indicates the fluorescence quenching of N-CQDs promoted the photocatalytic degradation of nicotine (Fig. 8
(a)-(d)). This was also demonstrated by the trapping agent experiments of N-CQDs after washing (Fig. 8 (e)). The k values of the washed N-CQDs were significantly lower than the N-CQDs before washing, suggesting that the fluorescence quenching of N-CQDs helps to improve their photocatalytic efficiency (Fig. 8 (f)). Moreover, in Fig. 3 (e), the photocatalytic activity of the N-CQDs catalyst improves with the increasing in nicotine concentration. In Fig. 4 (e)(f), the signals of ·O2 -and 1O2 radicals also increase with the increasing in nicotine concentration. These phenomena further prove that the promotion of the photocatalytic degradation after the fluorescence quenching of N-CQDs.

From all above results, the possible mechanism for the nicotine detection and degradation by N-CQDs under sunlight exposure is presented in Fig. 9 . The relevant band structure parameters are listed in Table S4 . N-CQDs emits blue-green fluorescence under solar light irradiation. The system is equipped with FRET conditions to transfer the fluorescence energy from the N-CQDs donor to the nicotinic acceptors after interacting with nicotine. A standard curve can be created by fluorescence intensity detection to determine the concentration of nicotine. After fluorescence quenching, N-CQDs can better take advantage of solar light for photocatalytic degradation. Under solar light irradiation, the N-CQDs photocatalyst can photogenerate electrons and holes from the VB to the CB, and ·O2 – can be produced because the N-CQDs photocatalyst CB potential is more negative than the O2/·O2 – (−0.33 eV) redox potential [51,52]. Meanwhile, photogenerated holes in the VB may have oxidized H2O to ·OH [53]. Photoelectron shuttled energy transfer between the excited-state three-state sensitizer and the ground-state molecular oxygen (3O2) is achieved, whereas 1O2 is created via the inter-system crossover (ISC) process [54]. ·O2 –, 1O2, ·OH and h+ all play major roles in the degradation of nicotine.

Although the target substrate can be efficiently removed by N-CQDs, transformation intermediates resulted during the photocatalytic degradation may be potential environmental pollutants. Therefore, the final products and the degradation intermediates of nicotine at different reaction times were analyzed using UPLC/HDMS (Figs. 10 and 11
) [3,55–57]. The retention time of nicotine in the positive mode is 2.41 min and the peak at m/z = 163.1235. Thirteen intermediates (A–M) are monitored in positive mode. Fig. S5 and S6 show the temporal change of these intermediates, the corresponding parameters are listed in Table S6 and S7 .

Firstly, the initial photodegradation product of nicotine may via the adipose ring losts the methyl group and dehydrogenated to produce intermediate B at m/z = 147.0922. At the same time, intermediate E is obtained though the adipose ring dehydrogenated to shape C=C double bond, Intermediate G is generated though the adipose ring oxidation to take shape C=O double bond. Intermediate B can be converted to its isomer C. Intermediate M is formed by ring opening, oxidation and hydroxylation of the adipose ring. Intermediate H is derived from the dehydrogenation of the M’s hydroxy. Intermediate J is received from the carboxylation reaction of H’s terminal group. Decarboxylation of J yields K. Intermediate E gives intermediate L by ring opening, oxidation and hydroxylation of the adipose ring. Thereafter, the various intermediates are further oxidized and decomposed.

Production pathways of B, C, E, L and J are consistent with the above low-concentration paths. Intermediate H is obtained by ring opening and oxidation of adipose ring. Intermediate I is derived from the double-hydroxylation reaction of H. Intermediate K can be received by I dedihydroxylation or J decarboxylation. Intermediate F is generated though Hydroxylationof the adipose ring. Intermediate G is achieved from the dehydrogenation of hydroxy. Intermediate D is emerged by ring opening, oxidation and hydroxylation of the adipose ring. Thereafter, Various intermediates are further oxidized and decomposed.

In this study, we construct N-CQDs with a high yield (33.7 %) via a facile hydrothermal treatment. The synthesized N-CQDs had better UV light absorption and faster photogenerated charge carrier separation and higher charge-transfer efficiency. Moreover, the N-CQDs system presented a higher degradation activity of nicotine with the increasing of nicotine concentration. The remarkable catalytic property of N-CQDs stemed from the formation of C-N-O groups during nitrogen doping at high temperature. This unique configuration could accelerate the separation efficiency of photogenerated electron and hole pairs to promote ·O2 – and 1O2 for fast pollutant oxidation. Furthermore, The N-CQDs could be served as fluorescent probe for the nicotine. Under optimal condition, the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs was linearly proportional with the nicotine concentration in the range of 1–50 mM, with LOD of 0.89 mM and LOQ of 2.71 mM. The notable performance of N-CQDs came from nitrogen doping of the circular structure suppresses C=N isomerization on the surface of CQDs, which enhances fluorescence property when interacting with specific nicotine guest molecules. In addition, N-CQDs is a CN-based material that is environmentally harmless after complete degradation. Therefore, our research provides a actuality strategy for detecting and removing tobacco by-products from the ecosystem.

Xiaoli Liu: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Conceptualization. Yaping Wang: Validation, Investigation. Yan Gu: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Wangyang Lu: Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.
